title: The Mysterious Founder Behind INSANE Rise of MidJourney

segments.text: Midjourney has become by far the best
way to make AI generated images. The
company makes tons of money even though
the team is really small. It's crazy how
quickly this has all happened. But this
overnight success story actually took
years to happen. Before David Holtz
became one of the most important leaders
in AI, he ran a company that never
really took off. And long before then,
he was a curious kid from South Florida.
David's dad was a dentist, but not your
average dentist. His office was on a
boat and he would sail around the
Caribbean in South America where he'd
pull up to port and do dental work for
people. Once David came along, his dad
settled down and rented out an office
near Fort Lauderdale. Because of his
job, David's dad had a computer. Of
course, David quickly became obsessed.
He learned how to program in this really
old language called Scheme. He even
started hacking computer games. One of
his favorites was this old DOSs game
called Star Wars Dark Forces. He ran a
design business while still in high
school. He attended tons of science
fairs and even ended up judging a few of
them. So, it probably didn't come as a
surprise to any of David's friends or
family when he decided to study physics
in college. David really admired the
great physicists, people like Einstein,
Newton, and Fineman. As he spent more
time in college, he also took lots of
math courses. He ended up graduating
with degrees in both and then started a
PhD in applied math. David was clearly
super smart and people were taking
notice. While he was doing his PhD, he
started working as a graduate researcher
at the Max Plank Institute, named after
the famous theoretical physicist. He
also worked for NASA at their Langley
campus. He was doing really cool stuff
like mapping rat brains, atmospheric
science, even working on LAR. But at a
certain point, David became worried that
he was getting locked into a career path
where he would just do research, write
papers, and then nothing really
happened. He wanted to make a bigger
impact. He wanted something that could
become his sole focus. So, David did
what lots of super smart, slightly
aimless people do. He started a company.
The company's first name was Oaspek, but
that wasn't a great name. So, David and
his co-founder soon changed it to Leap
Motion. The idea for Leapotion's main
product actually came to David back in
middle school. He was in a typing class
and he must have been bored because the
teacher told him if he could type more
than 60 words a minute without looking
at the keyboard, he didn't have to do
the class. So, David learned how to type
really fast. Got out of the class and
then all of a sudden he had time to work
on other projects. That experience of
learning how to type fast made David
realize that there was a problem with
how people interacted with technology. I
know most middle school students never
think about stuff like this. Basically,
he thought that there was a bottleneck.
People's minds worked really fast, but
seamlessly transmitting that information
was difficult. Cost, size, and speed are
all important factors. But for David,
the most important thing was the
interaction model. The end result of
that thinking was the Leap, a small
device you'd place in front of your
computer that would track the motion of
your hands and then translate those
gestures into commands. But well before
the Leap was released, in fact even
before David founded Lead Motion, he had
an interaction that foreshadowed the
debate we're having today about AI.
LeadMotion kind of invented a lot of
gestural interface technology, but
people weren't really ready. The iPad
had only just been released, and the
Windows operating system didn't yet work
with touchscreens at all. People didn't
really think about touch beyond their
phones and the trackpad on their
laptops. Of course, today touch is
everywhere and Apple recently announced
the Vision Pro, which includes hand
tracking is a core feature, but it's
taken more than 10 years after Leap
Motion's first product to get us here.
Because Leap Motion was trying to do
something new, David was very focused on
user interaction. One of the people he
talked to early on was a professor named
Alan K. Alan is a legendary computer
scientist who basically invented both
object-oriented programming and the
graphical user interface. and he said
something which crystallized the problem
David was trying to solve. Don't think
about features, think about how the user
learns. In many ways, Leap Motion never
really solved that problem. They tried
to introduce a new technological
paradigm before people were truly ready
for it. But today, user education both
in terms of people learning how to use
the technology and also how people train
the algorithm is a big part of why
Midjourney is so amazing. Leap Motion
had a promising first few years. Their
seed round in 2011 received investments
from Andre and Horowitz and Founders
Fund. But not every VC knew what
Leapotion was trying to do. Leap Motion,
however, followed a more conventional
path. After the successful seed funding
round, they raised $12.75 million in
series A funding in 2012 and then a $30
million series B round in 2013 led by
Founders Fund. That gave them the money
to release the leap in mid 2013. It sold
about 500,000 units. Apparently, that
was below expectations, but I think it's
actually pretty solid for a new hardware
company, selling a totally new type of
product right out of the gate. But there
were issues. Tech support was tricky.
User retention wasn't great, and that
sales figure was probably bumped up by
partnerships with other companies. in
addition to direct sales. Still, Leap
Motion was doing well enough that Apple
was interested in acquiring them, but
David, the founder and CTO, wasn't
interested. In fact, he apparently told
Apple's representatives that their
company was no longer innovative and
even praised Android. Needless to say,
the deal didn't go through, but still,
there's an interesting alternative
history here. At the time, Apple
actually acquired a few companies like
Prime Sense that had technology that
will end up being used in the Vision
Pro. Had Apple bought Leap Motion around
the same time, who knows how that would
have affected the creation of
Midjourney, or for that matter, the
Vision Pro. The company sent about
12,000 of its devices to developers who
were interested in building apps that
used handtracking technology. By now,
Leap Motion had pivoted away from the
desktop input of their first product and
toured virtual reality, but it wasn't
obvious how their tech would be
integrated into existing VR products.
There was an external kit you could add
on to the Oculus which would let you use
the Leap Motion controller, but that
just added a layer of complexity to a
device, which to be honest, people were
trying to wrap their heads around.
Anyway, in 2014, the Facebook bought
Oculus. After that, the VR industry sort
of cooled down a bit. But in 2018, a few
years after their first attempt, which
went so badly that David ended up
basically insulting them, Apple came
back and made another attempt to buy
Leap Motion. For her to buy Leap Motion,
this time was only around $30 to $50
million, which was just a fraction of
the $36 million valuation after the
series B round. It would have been a
massive drop in valuation. investors
would have only been getting cents on
the dollar and the founders and
employees probably would have received
basically nothing. Despite all that, the
deal almost happened. It even got to the
point where Apple sent out offer letters
to Leapotion employees who are happy
that they get the chance to keep working
on coolte from inside one of the world's
biggest companies. But it fell through
and we never got that version of
history. Around this time, Leap Motion,
which now had around 100 employees built
and open- sourced their augmented
reality headset, Northstar, underpinned
by their hand tracking technology. In
May of 2019, Leap Motion was bought by a
company called Ultra Haptics. The
financials weren't great. It was a $30
million deal, way down from the peak
valuation, so employees with stock
probably got wiped out, but from a
technological perspective, it made a lot
of sense. Ultrahaptics had been working
with David in Leap Motion for about 6
years by this point, and they worked on
interesting technology. Ultrahaptics
makes virtual control solutions that let
someone feel an object in their hands
when they interact with it in virtual
reality without needing to use gloves.
Today, Leap Motion is called Ultra Leap,
and it's unclear where their hand
control sensing technology is headed,
but it's growing. There are some cars
that let you turn up the volume on your
sound system by spinning your hand, but
in general, people aren't using their
hands to interact with computers just
yet. David ran Leap Motion for almost 10
years. He got a tons of experience
building new technology, raising money,
and growing a company from nothing into
something that Apple tried to buy twice.
It was time for the next adventure. He
set up a studio to explore new
opportunities. He told himself to wait
until the right one came up. As it
turned out, he wouldn't have to wait
very long. David Holtz chose to become
an entrepreneur instead of an academic
because he wanted to make a big impact.
So, this next part is kind of ironic
because an academic paper was about to
change his life. In June of 2020, three
Berkeley researchers, Jonathan Hoe, AJ
Jane, and Peter Abiel, published their
research into how diffusion models can
create images. David had enough
technical knowledge to understand that
diffusion models were about to increase
the quality of AI images by an order of
magnitude. He realized that the
opportunity he'd been waiting for had
arrived. The first version of OpenAI's
DAL model proved him right, but in
reverse. Today, Deli 2 is one of the
leading AI image generators, but when
the first one was released in January of
2021, not that many people actually paid
attention. DAL 1 was the first textto
image model based on diffusion models
that existed. It was a step change from
everything that came before. The DAL 1
launch also include a package called
clip which could evaluate how well an
image aligned with text inputs. Plenty
of AI researchers who tried to build
image generators before but using older
methods like generative adversarial
networks and variational autoenccoders.
People who were paying close attention
like David was knew that this was a
turning point. Sam Alman, the head of
OpenA, I wasn't exactly sitting around
either. Dalot 2 was released in April of
2022. Stable diffusion followed in
August and Midjourney entered open beta
in July. David Holtz was actually
experimenting with the preliminary
Midjourney model as far back as January
before Dalle 2 dropped, doing things
like creating vapor wave monkeys in the
MidJourney Discord. The Discord went
public a couple months later. This was
actually a really important milestone
because it allowed David and his team to
go really hard on user testing. For
example, one model took about 20 minutes
to create a really highquality image,
while another took about only 15
seconds, but produced worse images. The
first approach was really fast, but it
sacrificed quality. The second approach
was resulted in amazing images, but
users would often get bored. In the end,
David found a great midpoint. Today, it
takes midjourney about 60 seconds to
generate an image, and it shows you the
image as it's generating. The hardest
thing about building an AI company today
is the intense public scrutiny. You've
got Sam Alman in front of Congress and
lots of people who literally believe
that programs like ChatGpt should be
illegal. But despite that pressure,
David's built MidJourney into the best
AI image generator around. And he's done
it by implementing four distinct
strategies. Number one, harnessing the
power of Discord. You can measure the
growth of most companies by looking at
their revenues, but the best way to
measure the growth of MidJourney is by
looking at their Discord. If you go on
sites like Reddit, you'll see the same
question pop up again and again. Why did
the Midjourney team decide to wrap their
product inside a Discord server? It's a
fair question. Frankly, lots of tech
CEOs would be too skittish to ever do
something like that. But David
immediately understood that Discord
could be an amazing feedback loop. He
believed that one of the key drivers to
Midjourney's massive growth would be
virality. And then one of the best ways
to create virality is volume. Today,
Midjourney is the world's biggest
Discord server by far. It hit 2 million
subscribers in midsepptember 2022, 4
million by the second week of November.
By April, it hit 15 million. And
currently there are more than 18 million
members. That's exponential growth. The
more people in the Discord, the more
people creating amazing images. And the
more amazing images you see, then the
more likely you are to want to join the
Discord. Discord just makes sense. It's
flexible and functional. It has a great
mobile app, and it's collaborative. One
of the biggest threats to the growth of
a generative AI model is that it's
actually really hard to come up with a
good prompt, especially by yourself.
People aren't normally very creative by
themselves. They might ask Midjourney to
do something like create an image of a
dog and after some encouragement, they
might put in terms like fluffy dog or
big fluffy dog. But on the MidJourney
Discord, you can literally ask 18
million other people for ideas. So, you
end up with cooler prompts and you're
more likely to keep using Midjourney.
The Discord also acts as a sounding
board and decision-making forum. The
moderators hold town halls where people
can advocate for the inclusion or
exclusion of certain words or phrases.
There are tiers and some words can only
be used for private images and it's
where the rules are made and enforced.
Users aren't allowed to create deep
fakes. Not safe for work content is
generally banned and you're not allowed
to create images of controversial
political figures like Xiinping. Number
two, product market fit. Like lots of
people in tech, David has a big bias for
action. He has a really interesting
quote about it. Many of my brilliant
friends are paralyzed by their
intellects thinking overwhelms doing.
Many of my successful friends are less
reflective but quick to action. They are
always focused on moving forward, making
as many probably correct actions as
possible a winning strategy. This quote
reminds me of his struggles about
whether or not to stay in academia. He
probably met lots of people like that,
super smart people who needed things to
be perfect. But David realized he was
different. And that difference, that
bias to action is really important in
AI. Even by the standards of software,
generative AI is moving insanely fast.
There isn't time to hone something to
perfection. Instead, David releases a
minimum viable product, uses
Midjourney's massive, superengaged user
base to make it better, and keeps
iterating. It's modern software
development at top speed. And David is
taking a very different approach from
other AI companies. Lots of AI companies
are fighting right now to become an
infrastructure layer and don't want to
focus on building a consumer product.
They want to build a platform. You know
what that reminds me of? It reminds me
of when David, when he was still at Leap
Motion, sent thousands of handtracking
devices to developers so they could
develop apps. And it reminds me of VR
headsets that have really amazing
hardware, but no killer app. Everyone
wants to create a platform because
that's where the big monetization
opportunities are. But if you don't
create a great product, then the
platform doesn't matter. Leap Motion
never really took off because despite
having cool tech, it didn't give enough
people a reason to use it. Midjourney is
different. Artists use it to improve
their practice, but mostly tons of
regular people use it because they're
curious to see what it can do. If you
make your available market everyone
online and then make your product
incredibly easy and fun to use, then
you're going to get great product market
fit. Number three, focusing on need.
Right now, the era of low interest rates
and easy VC money feels like a long time
ago. A company that makes hardware based
on speculative technology like Leap
Motion might not get off the ground. It
probably wouldn't raise $100 million.
I'm not saying David could see where
interest rates were going when he
started MidJourney, but he did make a
deliberate choice to totally bootstrap
the company. David was a known quantity
in Silicon Valley. He probably could
have gone out and raised lots of money
for anything. Instead, he did something
way better. He consulted his years of
experience and asked himself, "What is
the scarce resource I need more of to
truly scale?" The answer wasn't actually
money. It was compute. So, when
MidJourney was just starting out, he
leveraged his network. He did stuff like
email cloud vendors and asked to lock
down access to GPUs to scale his
service. It's not just about capacity
either. David remembered that in the
early days of the Discord, some people
were getting frustrated with how long it
would take to generate high-quality
images. So today, Midjourney actively
searches for GPUs in parts of the world
where it's night in order to accelerate
the process. That dynamic load balancing
is what's enabled it to consistently
keep rendering times pretty low. David's
experience shows that you don't
necessarily need to wait until interest
rates go down again to start a company.
You just need to identify what you need
most and then focus on obtaining as much
of it as you
can. Number four, the value of learning.
Midjourney gets better by learning from
its past experiences. So did David. He
spent almost a decade running Leap
Motion and before that he spent years in
academia. He's been an active member of
the AI community absorbing the technical
knowledge and the philosophical debates.
All of those experiences made him a
better founder, CEO, and engineer. And
just as importantly, David isn't done
learning. He holds 4-hour long sessions
with thousands of people on Discord to
answer questions and see how everyone's
doing. He's constantly tinkering with
Midjourney itself and looking for ways
to make it a better product. And he's
still constantly thinking about the
place of AI in our society and how it
can contribute to making us better. If
you enjoyed this video, don't forget to
hit that like button, subscribe to the
channel, and click the bell so you don't
miss out on more incredible stories like
this one. Thanks for watching.
